elements.sent-2411-001: A second-order-accurate finite volume method, hybridized by blending an extended double-flux algorithm and a traditionally conservative scheme, is developed.
elements.sent-2411-002: In this scheme, hybrid convective fluxes as well as hybrid interpolation techniques are designed to ensure stability and accuracy in the presence of both material interfaces and shocks.
elements.sent-2411-003: Two computationally efficient approaches, extended from the original double-flux model, are presented to eliminate the well-known "pressure oscillation" phenomenon at material interfaces observed with the traditional conservative scheme.
elements.sent-2411-004: Numerous verfication simulations confirm that the method is capable of handling multi-dimensional shock-interface problems reliably and efficiently, even in the presence of viscous and reactive terms.
elements.sent-2411-005: It is nowadays well established that a fully conservative scheme will usually lead to spurious pressure and velocity oscillations at material interfaces with variable specific heat ratio $\gamma$ when solving the Euler or Navier-Stokes equations in the hyperbolic regime~\cite{larrouturou1991preserve,karni1994multicomponent,abgrall1996prevent,ton1996improved .}
elements.sent-2411-006: Earlier works by Karni \cite{karni1994multicomponent and Jenny et al. \cite{jenny1997correction recognized that this pressure oscillation problem is rooted in the numerical averaging or smearing of $\gamma$ across cells.}}
elements.sent-2411-007: To eliminate this issue, one way is to develop "sharp-interface" methods, which essentially avoid smearing out $\gamma$ by tracking the propagating material front~\cite{ton1996improved,shyue1998efficient,johnson2020conservative .}
elements.sent-2411-008: One classic method among them was presented by Karni \cite{karni1994multicomponent who evolved the original governing equations into a non-conservative formulation by solving additionally the advection equation of one primitive variable.}
elements.sent-2411-009: Using this approach, artificial pressure oscillations were perfectly cured and Abgrall \cite{abgrall1996prevent later extended this approach to handle strong shocks as well.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Besides, there is also a family of non-oscillatory approaches named as the "Ghost Fluid Method" (GFM) \cite{fedkiw1997high,fedkiw1998efficient,fedkiw1999non,fedkiw2002coupling , which preserve the interface sharpness by defining a level-set function and using ghost fluid cells to swap information across the interface.}
elements.sent-2411-011: The GFM methods combine the advantages of both Eulerian and Langrangian approaches and have been widely used to handle multi-phase fluid problems~\cite{chang2013direct,luo2015conservative,xu2022interface .}

elements.section-2411-004: An efficient, adaptive solver for simulation of shock-interface interaction
elements.sent-2411-151: In spite of our efforts, the inherent conservation concerns accompanied with the extended double-flux method remain an issue.
elements.sent-2411-152: Hence, we have designed an explicit hybrid methodology that ensures correct shock capturing nevertheless.
elements.sent-2411-153: In addition, we have incorporated a highly efficient adaptive mesh refinement (AMR) technique into the overall solver.
elements.sent-2411-154: In compressible flows, shock waves are ubiquitous, e.g., in shock bubble interaction, shock induced combustion or detonation.
elements.sent-2411-155: In that respect, a fully conservative shock capturing method is usually preferred under those circumstances to make sure one obtains the correct weak solution.
elements.sent-2411-156: Using a non-conservative scheme like the double-flux method, on the other hand, is generally not a good choice because it might generate huge numerical artifacts by producing an unphysical shock.
elements.sent-2411-157: To remedy this deficiency, we apply hybridized numerical fluxes in the present study by combining the fully conservative shock-capturing scheme and the quasi-conservative extended double-flux method.
elements.sent-2411-158: To achieve this, we utilize the normalised pressure curvature \cite{hill2004hybrid as a shock sensor \begin{equation}S_{i} = \frac{\left|\bar{p}_{i-1}-2 \bar{p}_{i}+\bar{p}_{i+1}\right|}{\bar{p}_{i-1}+2 \bar{p}_{i}+\bar{p}_{i+1}}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-159: This normalized sensor is generally resolution independent and hence directly compatible with the local grid refinement of the AMR technique employed.
elements.sent-2411-160: The working procedure is as follows and summarized in Algorithm \ref{al1 .}
elements.sent-2411-161: Before time marching each computational cell, the sensor value $S_{i}$ is calculated to determine which scheme (conservative or non-conservative) is selected.
elements.sent-2411-162: Once $S_{i}$ exceeds a prescribed threshold $S_{i ^{t}$, the double-flux solver will reduce back to the traditional conservative solver by jumping over the frozen and flux correction procedures.}
elements.sent-2411-163: If not, a quasi-conservative scheme (aka the extended double-flux ) would be applied on this cell with the use of either $Approach A$ or $B$.
elements.sent-2411-164: After that, a correction procedure is implemented to update the thermodynamic quantities as well as the total energy $\rho E$.
elements.sent-2411-165: Determination of the threshold $S_{i ^{t}$ is naturally problem-related, which is a common issue of hybrid methods, cf.. \cite{ziegler2011adaptive .}}
elements.sent-2411-166: In most occasions, they can be resolved separately with the use of our presented hybrid solver.
elements.sent-2411-167: For phenomena like the detonation wave in whose structure the shock and reaction fronts are coupled closely, it should be and also is resolved simply by the conservative scheme since this phenomenon is dominated more by shock compression instead of physical diffusion and thereby ensuring the correct weak solution is critical.
elements.sent-2411-168: The extended double-flux scheme is a Godunov-type scheme and is usually extended to higher order accuracy in space and time \cite{billet2003adaptive .}
elements.sent-2411-169: For the MUSCL-TVD scheme employed in this work, we interpolate the primitive variables onto the cell edges, which is computationally efficient and preferred in most occasions.
elements.sent-2411-170: However, as reported in \cite{houim2011low , this interpolation technique may generate oscillations near shock fronts.}
elements.sent-2411-171: Although this can be tackled to some degree by decomposing the interpolation variables onto the characteristic fields globally, the entire computational load would be tremendously increased and also the double-flux algorithms would become much more involved \cite{houim2011low .}

elements.sent-2411-274: The present study has successfully advanced a hybrid methodology within the finite-volume method, achieving a verified precision of second-order accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-275: The proposed new hybrid solver merges an extended double-flux strategy with conventional conservative methodologies, thereby ensuring enhanced stability and accuracy in multi-dimensional scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-276: Central to this solver are the carefully tailored hybrid schemes for managing convective fluxes and interpolations.
elements.sent-2411-277: These designs guarantee optimal performance in maintaining solution stability while simultaneously delivering high-fidelity results, even under challenging conditions that combine material interfaces with shock discontinuities.
elements.sent-2411-278: To extend the original double-flux technique, this paper put forth two full computational approaches, which have both proven reliable and efficient in suppressing pressure oscillations occurring at the material interfaces and at the same time require only one auxillary variable $\hat{\gamma}$, with tiny difference in momentum conservation preservation.
elements.sent-2411-279: Extensive validations have conclusively demonstrated the efficiency and resilience of our solver in tackling multi-dimensional, shock-interface challenges, even in scenarios complicated by viscosity and chemical reactions by combining with the adaptive mesh refinement (AMR) technique.

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Hybridization methodology} \label{al1} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \STATE Calculate and store cell-based auxiliary variable $\hat{\gamma}$ using either Eq. \ref{eq27} or Eq. \ref{eq32}. \STATE Compute the troubled-cell indicator $S_{k}$ for shock detection ($> S_{k}^{T}$ using shock-capturing scheme, $> S_{k}^{T}$ with extended double-flux scheme). \IF {$S_{k} < S_{k}^{T}$} \STATE Save $\boldsymbol{U^n}$ first \STATE First RK Stage: \STATE Interpolate primitive variables onto cell edges; calculate inconsistent numerical fluxes using the extended double-flux scheme with the addition of viscous fluxes \STATE Update state vector $\boldsymbol{U^n}$ with dual edge fluxes to obtain $\boldsymbol{U^*}$. \STATE Update primitive variables as follows: \STATE $Y_i^* = \frac{(\rho Y_i)^*}{\sum (\rho Y_i)^*}$, $u^* = \frac{(\rho u)^*}{\sum (\rho Y_i)^*}$, $v^* = \frac{(\rho v)^*}{\sum (\rho Y_i)^*}$ \STATE $p^* = \left(\hat{\gamma}-1\right)\left((\rho E)^* - (\rho h_{0}^f)^* - \frac{\rho^* \boldsymbol{u}^{*2}}{2}\right)$, $T^* = \frac{p^*}{RU\sum \frac{(\rho Y_i)^*}{W_i}}$ \STATE Second RK Stage: \STATE Repeat steps 6-10 for updating of state vector $\boldsymbol{U^*}$ to get the final state $\boldsymbol{U^{n+1}}$. \STATE Update thermodynamic variables: \STATE $(C_{pi}^*)^{n+1} = C_{pi}^*(T^{n+1})$, $(\hat{\gamma})^{n+1} = \frac{C_{p}^*}{C_{p}^*-R}$ \STATE Correct the total energy $(\rho E)^{n+1}$: \STATE $(\rho E)^{n+1} = \frac{p^{n+1}}{(\hat{\gamma})^{n+1} -1} + (\rho h_0^f)^{n+1} + \frac{(\rho \boldsymbol{u}^2)^{n+1} }{2}$ \ELSE \STATE Save $\boldsymbol{U^n}$ first \STATE First RK Stage: \STATE Interpolate charatertistic variables onto cell edges; calculate conservative numerical fluxes using the conservative scheme with the addition of viscous fluxes \STATE Update state vector $\boldsymbol{U^n}$ with single edge flux to obtain $\boldsymbol{U^*}$. \STATE Second RK Stage: \STATE Repeat steps 20-21 for updating of $\boldsymbol{U^*}$ to get the final state $\boldsymbol{U^{n+1}}$. \ENDIF \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-288: The slope obtained in each characteristic field can then be projected back to the component space by \begin{eqnarray}\Delta \boldsymbol{Q}_{i}= \boldsymbol{R}_{i} \Delta W_{i}.\end{eqnarray}.
elements.sent-2411-289: Finally, the left and right values at the interface can be acquired by \begin{eqnarray}\boldsymbol{Q}_{i+1 / 2}^{L}=\boldsymbol{Q}^{l}_{i}+\frac{1}{2} \Delta \boldsymbol{Q}_{i}, \boldsymbol{Q}_{i-1 / 2}^{R}=\boldsymbol{Q}^{l}_{i}-\frac{1}{2} \Delta \boldsymbol{Q}_{i}.\end{eqnarray}.
